entry.title: Budapest, the City That History Keeps Rewriting

link.title: Budapest, the City That History Keeps Rewriting

entry.content:
The Budapest skyline at sunset from Gellert Hill - Buda on the left, Pest on the right.


There’s a moment, usually at dusk and usually from the Buda side of the river, when Budapest reveals itself all at once. The Neo-Gothic spires of Parliament catch the last amber rays of light that sparkle across the Danube. The Chain Bridge arches between its famous stolid stone lions, the elegant and eclectic cafes of Pest spill out onto the embankment, and you understand why they call this city the “Paris of the East”.

But Budapest refuses to be a simple fascimile of its western cousin. Where Paris’ Seine divides similar neighborhoods, the Danube divides two cities that feel genuinely distinct. Buda, medieval and hilly, climbing toward a castle that has watched empires rise and fall myriad times; and Pest, flat, urban, and dynamic, its grand Austro-Hungarian boulevards bisected by a warren of ruin pubs, literary cafés, and a nightlife that runs until dawn. Together they form one of Europe’s most layered and surprising capitals — a city shaped by Celts, Romans, Huns, Magyar tribesman, Ottomans, Habsburgs, Nazis, Soviets, and its modern Hungarian residents.

Come here to soak in tiled bathhouses that have been warm since the Romans, to drink in a converted war ruins, to stand before a parliament building so beautiful it seems to belong in a dream, and to be surprised by a city that has survived everything and somehow come out more itself.


Table of Contents

  • A City Born from Three
  • Where to Stay
  • Monuments & Architecture
  • Hidden Gems
  • Museums
  • Parks & Outdoor Walks
  • The Baths
  • Eat & Drink
  • Getting Around & Practical Notes


A Short History of Budapest

Budapest’s history is the history of this part of Europe in miniature: ancient, violent, beautiful, and deeply human.

The Celts were here first, drawn by what they called Ak-Ink — “abundant water” — the geothermal springs bubbling up from a geological fault beneath the city. Around 100 CE, the Romans arrived, recognized a strategic prize, and built Aquincum on the Danube’s bank, eventually making it the capital of the province of Pannonia Inferior. For four centuries this was a proper Roman city of 30,000 people — with amphitheaters, thermal baths, public latrines, and all the infrastructure of empire. You can still walk those ruins today in the Óbuda district. The Romans left their most enduring gift not in stone but in habit: the first formal bath culture, drawing from those same geothermal springs that still warm the city’s great baths today.

Then came the Huns, then the Magyars, arriving from the east in 895 CE and planting the roots of the Hungarian nation. Then the Ottomans, who occupied Buda from 1541 to 1686 — long enough to leave their own indelible mark in domed hammams and octagonal soaking pools that still define the city’s bath architecture. The Habsburgs followed, rebuilding after the wars of reconquest and transforming Budapest into a grand imperial outpost. When Buda, Óbuda, and Pest officially unified in 1873, the new capital threw itself into a building boom that produced Parliament, the Opera House, the grand boulevards, and most of what you will see and photograph today.

The 20th century was less kind. WWI ended Hungary’s imperial ambitions. WWII brought destruction, siege, and the Holocaust — scars still visible in the bullet holes on some facades, in the shoes along the Danube riverbank, in the weight of the House of Terror museum. Then decades of Soviet rule left a complicated legacy: the statues that now stand in exile at Memento Park, the underground bunker hospitals, the secret police files. Modern Budapest has absorbed all of it and made something remarkable from the ruins. The ruin pubs of District VII — bars opened in abandoned buildings — are a perfect metaphor for the whole city: creative reinvention of whatever history left behind.

Where to Stay

Budapest’s districts each have their own character. Choose one that matches how you want to spend your days:

  • District V (Belváros–Lipótváros) — Upscale and central. Near Parliament and the Danube promenade, ideal for first-timers who want everything within walking distance.
  • District VII (Jewish Quarter) — The most atmospheric choice. Ruin pubs, street food, the Grand Synagogue, and a nightlife that doesn’t stop. A neighborhood that rewards wandering at any hour.
  • District VI (Terézváros) — Stylish and literary, centered on Andrássy Avenue, Budapest’s World Heritage boulevard. Close to the Opera House and excellent cafés.
  • District I (Castle Hill) — Historic and scenic, with cobblestone lanes and the best elevated views in the city. Quieter at night, magical in the early morning before the crowds arrive.
  • District VIII (Palace District) — Quieter, more local, with beautiful old palaces being slowly restored and excellent neighborhood cafés.
  • Óbuda — The most residential, with Roman ruins and far less tourist traffic than the rest. A good base if you want to live more like a local.
Budapest's central districts — the highlighted core contains most of what you'll want to explore on foot.

Monuments & Architecture

Hungarian Parliament Building

The Parliament is the center of gravity of the whole city — an impossibly grand Neo-Gothic structure completed in 1904 after 17 years of construction, one of Europe’s oldest and largest legislative buildings. Its riverfront profile, with the dome and twin towers reflected in the Danube, is the image that defines Budapest. Visit on a guided tour to see the Hungarian Crown Jewels inside; visit from the Buda side at sunset to see it at its most spectacular.

The Hungarian Parliament Building illuminated at night, its reflection spreading across the Danube.
Parliament from the water — the view from a Danube river cruise captures the full scale of the building.

Buda Castle & Fisherman’s Bastion

Perched atop Castle Hill, Buda Castle has anchored this city’s skyline since the 13th century, though the current Baroque palace was largely rebuilt after WWII. The complex houses the Hungarian National Gallery, the Budapest History Museum, and the National Library.

The courtyard of Buda Castle — the Baroque palace complex that has presided over the city for centuries.

Just outside the castle gates, the neo-Romanesque Fisherman’s Bastion offers the most photographed view in Budapest — seven towers symbolizing the seven Magyar tribes, overlooking the Danube and Parliament. Come before 10pm while the lights are still on, and linger in the early morning before the day-trippers arrive.

Fisherman's Bastion dusted with winter snow — the towers and the Chain Bridge visible in the valley below.

Chain Bridge

Budapest’s first permanent Danube crossing, finished in 1849, the Chain Bridge was designed by English engineer William Tierney Clark and built by Scottish engineer Adam Clark — a modern wonder for its time. Its famous stone lions stand guard at each end (the tongue, famously said to be missing, is merely not visible from street level). Named after Count István Széchenyi, the man most responsible for the city’s 19th-century transformation, this bridge is where Buda and Pest truly became one.

The Chain Bridge at night — one of Budapest's stone lions stands watch as the bridge lights illuminate the Danube.

The Opera House

The Hungarian State Opera House on Andrássy Avenue was built to rival Vienna’s — and many visitors claim it succeeds. Designed by Miklós Ybl and opened in 1884 with Emperor Franz Joseph in attendance, its interior is laden with frescoes by Károly Lotz, gold leaf, and a 3-ton chandelier. The acoustics, shaped by the horseshoe hall design, are considered among the finest in Europe. Even if you don’t catch a performance, the guided tours are exceptional.

The gilded horseshoe hall of the Hungarian State Opera House — frescoed ceiling, tiered balconies, and a chandelier weighing over three tons.

Heroes’ Square & Andrássy Avenue

Heroes’ Square (Hősök tere) marks the ceremonial end of Andrássy Avenue — Budapest’s grand boulevard and UNESCO World Heritage site. The Millennium Monument at its center commemorates the 1,000th anniversary of the Magyar conquest of the Carpathian Basin, with Archangel Gabriel atop a tall column surrounded by the seven founding chieftains. The square is flanked by the Museum of Fine Arts and the Palace of Art. Allow a full afternoon to walk the length of Andrássy Avenue, stopping at the Opera House, the palatial townhouses, and the Liszt Ferenc Memorial Museum along the way.

Heroes' Square — the Millennium Monument, Archangel Gabriel, and the colonnades of Hungary's founding chieftains.

Gellért Hill & the Citadel

Rising 235 meters above the Danube, Gellért Hill is where the city’s patron saint was martyred in the 11th century — thrown in a barrel — and from which the Habsburgs watched over the city from their Citadel after the 1848–49 Hungarian Revolution. Today the hill offers the finest panoramic views in Budapest. The Liberty Statue at its summit, erected in 1947, still presides over the city with its palm branch raised toward the sky.

Budapest at dusk from Gellért Hill — Buda Castle on the left, the Chain Bridge at center, and Parliament glowing on the right bank.

Hidden Gems

Cave Church

Hidden in the rock face of Gellért Hill, this small atmospheric church was carved directly into the rock in the 1920s, inspired by Lourdes in France. The entrance looks like a cave opening marked with a wooden cross — easy to miss, impossible to forget. During communist times it was sealed with concrete and the monks imprisoned; reopened after 1989, it offers one of Budapest’s most quietly powerful spiritual spaces.

Inside the Cave Church — a shrine carved into the living rock of Gellért Hill, sealed during communism and reopened after 1989.

Hospital in the Rock

Beneath Castle Hill lies a labyrinth of caves converted into an emergency hospital during WWII and later adapted as a Cold War nuclear bunker. Now a museum, it preserves original equipment and uses wax figures to recreate the experience of wartime medicine underground. The air inside is still heavy; parts of the exhibition faintly smell of disinfectant. Unsettling and essential.

The Hospital in the Rock — a WWII emergency hospital cut into the caves beneath Castle Hill, preserved exactly as it was.

Vajdahunyad Castle

Built for Hungary’s millennial exhibition in 1896, Vajdahunyad is an architectural fantasy — a pastiche of Gothic towers, Renaissance courtyards, Baroque wings, and Romanesque arches, all modeled on famous Hungarian buildings from across the country. It looks centuries old but is mostly theatrical invention. Surrounded by parkland with a moat, it’s particularly beautiful in summer and autumn.

Vajdahunyad Castle reflected in the City Park lake — a Gothic-Renaissance-Baroque pastiche that looks far older than 1896.

Zwack Unicum Museum

Unicum is Hungary’s national spirit — a bitter herbal digestif made from a secret recipe of more than 40 herbs, produced by the Zwack family since 1790. The museum in their historic distillery tells the story of how the recipe was smuggled to the West during communism. The cellar tour ends with a tasting amid aging oak casks. History, branding, and decent schnapps.

The Zwack Unicum Museum — the historic distillery where Hungary's national spirit has been made since 1790.

Ecseri Flea Market

On the city’s outskirts, Ecseri is a sprawling treasure hunt: communist memorabilia, vintage cameras, strange porcelain, oil paintings, Soviet-era passports, propaganda posters, furniture of every decade. Film set designers source props here. Arrive early on a weekend morning, bring cash, expect to haggle. One of Budapest’s great adventures if you’re willing to leave the center.

Ecseri Flea Market — antiques, Soviet curiosities, and decades of Hungarian life stacked in a sprawling weekend market on the city's edge.

Museums

Hungarian National Museum — Hungary’s oldest public museum (founded 1802), housed in a neoclassical building that became the symbolic birthplace of the 1848 Revolution, when poet Sándor Petőfi read his National Song from the front steps. The collection spans 1,000 years of Hungarian history from Roman Pannonia through the Habsburg era. The medieval coronation regalia and Carpathian Basin goldwork are standouts.

The Hungarian National Museum — founded in 1802, its neoclassical steps became the stage for the 1848 Revolution.

House of Terror Museum — Located in the former headquarters of both the Fascist Arrow Cross and the communist secret police (ÁVH), this museum documents the double occupation of Hungary in the 20th century. The basement, where prisoners were held and executed, is particularly difficult. Budapest does not look away from its history.

CAPA Institute — Dedicated to the photography of Robert Capa, the Hungarian-Jewish photographer who became the most celebrated war photojournalist of the 20th century. A small, beautifully curated museum in District V. Unmissable.

Museum of Fine Arts (MFAB) — The grand neoclassical building at Heroes’ Square houses one of Central Europe’s strongest art collections, with major holdings in old master paintings, Egyptian artifacts, and European drawings.

House of Music — A striking building in City Park designed by Japanese architect Sou Fujimoto, with a canopy roof that mimics a forest canopy. An interactive music museum as interesting for its architecture as for its exhibits.

Dohány Street Synagogue — The largest synagogue in Europe, and one of the most moving buildings in Budapest. The memorial garden behind it, where thousands of Budapest Jews were buried during the wartime siege, is a place of genuine solemnity. Do not miss it.

Parks & Outdoor Walks

Margaret Island — A car-free island in the middle of the Danube, Budapest’s favorite place to breathe. Locals jog its 5km circuit, picnic under ancient trees, and rent bikes. There are medieval monastery ruins, a small zoo, a musical fountain, and thermal pools. Named after Princess Margaret, daughter of King Béla IV, who lived in a convent here in the 13th century. A perfect antidote to heavy sightseeing.

The musical fountain on Margaret Island — Budapest's beloved car-free escape in the middle of the Danube.

Memento Park — After communism fell in 1989, Budapest removed its Soviet statues. Rather than destroying them, they were relocated here — a strange park on the city’s outskirts filled with massive Lenin figures, heroic workers with flags, and Stalin’s boots (the rest of the statue was torn down by the people in 1956). The effect is surreal and sobering: a cemetery for an ideology. The small museum with communist-era secret police training films is darkly fascinating.

Memento Park — where Budapest exiled its Soviet-era statues after 1989. A surreal open-air museum of communist ideology.

Kerepesi Cemetery — Often compared to Père Lachaise in Paris, Kerepesi is a sprawling park cemetery with elaborate tombs and mausoleums of statesmen, poets, composers, and revolutionary leaders. Some graves are works of art in themselves — towering stone angels, weeping sculptures, ornate crypts. A quiet, reflective place where Hungarian history feels palpable underfoot.

Kerepesi Cemetery in autumn — a tree-lined boulevard leading through one of Europe's great historic burial grounds.

The Andrássy Avenue Walk — Walk the full length of Andrássy Avenue from Deák Ferenc Square to Heroes’ Square. Stop at the Opera House, duck into the side streets, notice the neo-Renaissance architecture of the townhouses, and end at one of the grand cafés near the square. UNESCO heritage status well deserved.

The Baths

Budapest sits atop a geological fault that feeds more than 100 thermal springs beneath the city. The Romans built the first baths here; the Ottomans refined the culture and added their domed architecture; and today the thermal bath remains one of the city’s most distinctive and pleasurable experiences — part spa, part social institution, part living museum.

Széchenyi Thermal Bath — The largest and most famous, opened in 1913, with three outdoor pools and 15 indoor pools fed by two thermal springs rich in calcium, magnesium, and hydrogen carbonate. Its Neo-Baroque yellow palace architecture is half the draw. The outdoor pools, where locals famously play chess while soaking, are an iconic Budapest image. Go in the morning or evening to avoid the midday tourist surge.

Széchenyi Thermal Bath — the iconic yellow Neo-Baroque palace and its famous outdoor pools, open year-round.

Rudas Thermal Bath — Built in the 16th century during Ottoman occupation, Rudas has the most atmospheric traditional bathing space in the city: a central octagonal pool under a massive Turkish dome with colored glass skylights that filter the light into something otherworldly. Soaking here feels like time travel. The rooftop hot tub offers panoramic Danube views, especially magical at sunset. Rudas still maintains separate men-only and women-only days for the traditional baths — a custom observed for 500 years.

Rudas Thermal Bath — the 16th-century Ottoman dome and its octagonal pool, unchanged in 500 years of continuous use.

Gellért Thermal Bath — Part of the landmark Hotel Gellért, these Art Nouveau baths opened in 1918 and are the most ornate in the city, with mosaics, stained glass, and sculptures throughout. A more luxurious and architecturally spectacular experience than Széchenyi, with smaller crowds. The outdoor wave pool in summer is a Budapest institution.

Gellért Thermal Bath — Art Nouveau columns, warm mineral pools, and an atmosphere that belongs to another century.

Practical note: shower before entering the pools, bring flip-flops and a towel (or rent them at the bath). Most baths require swimwear.

Eat & Drink

Cheap Good Eats

Budapest is a deeply affordable city for food, especially if you explore beyond the tourist corridors.

  • Central Market Hall (Nagy Vásárcsarnok) — Budapest’s great iron-and-tile covered market, built in 1897. The ground floor offers fresh produce, paprika, salami, and foie gras at excellent prices. The upper floor has hot Hungarian food stalls and souvenirs. Come hungry on a weekday morning when the market is at its liveliest.
The Central Market Hall — Budapest's great covered market since 1897, with paprika, salami, foie gras, and everything Hungarian under one iron roof.
  • Szimpla Farmers’ Market — On Sunday mornings, the city’s most famous ruin pub transforms into a lively farmers’ market with cheese, honey, sausages, and freshly baked bread. Street musicians, families, and tourists mixing in a space that was a nightclub hours earlier. One of the best ways to see how Budapest mixes old and new.
The Sunday farmers' market at Szimpla Kert — honey, sausages, and fresh produce sold in a space that's a packed nightclub by evening.
  • Lángos — Fried bread, traditionally served with sour cream and cheese, though you’ll find it topped with everything imaginable. The definitive Budapest street food. Try A Lángosos for a local favorite, or look for stands at any market.
  • Karaván Street Food Court — Outdoor food trucks and stalls right next to Szimpla Kert, good for a quick and varied lunch.
  • Bors GasztroBár — Famous for its soups and inventive gourmet sandwiches. Tiny, often with a line. Worth it.
  • Kádár Étkezde — Old-school Hungarian canteen in the Jewish Quarter, serving honest Budapest comfort food to a mix of regulars and knowing visitors. Cash only, no pretense.
  • Gettó Gulyás — Affordable and excellent, specializing in stews and goulash the way they should taste.
  • Menza — Retro-inspired Hungarian food in a fun, busy atmosphere. Reliable for both lunch and dinner.

Local dishes to try: goulash (a rich paprika-forward beef stew, nothing like the canned version), chimney cake (kürtőskalács — spiral pastry rolled in sugar and nuts), and foie gras (Hungary produces some of the world’s finest, at a fraction of French prices).

Fine Dining

Budapest’s restaurant scene has caught international attention in recent years, with a new generation of chefs reinterpreting Hungarian cuisine with fresh ambition.

  • Costes Downtown — Michelin-starred, with seasonal tasting menus that showcase Hungary’s exceptional produce. The benchmark for fine dining in the city.
  • Borkonyha Winekitchen — Michelin-starred wine kitchen where the food and the cellar are equals. Outstanding Hungarian wines with each course.
  • Onyx — High-end refined Hungarian gastronomy in a sophisticated setting.
  • Stand — A celebrated modern take on traditional Hungarian dishes; more relaxed than Costes but just as considered.
  • Babel Budapest — Inventive tasting menus with a focus on local ingredients and unexpected technique.
  • Gundel — Budapest’s legendary restaurant since 1894, serving Hungarian classics in the City Park setting it has occupied for over a century. Historic dining at its most graceful.
  • Rosenstein — Classic Jewish-Hungarian cuisine with a distinguished wine list. A famous Budapest institution.
  • Déryné — Elegant brasserie in a beautiful setting; excellent for a long weekend lunch.
  • Dobrumba — Israeli and Mediterranean cooking, vivid and unlike anything else in the Budapest dining scene.
  • Essencia — A Portuguese-Hungarian hybrid that sounds unlikely and tastes extraordinary.

Ruin Bars & Nightlife

The ruin pub is Budapest’s great contribution to nightlife culture. Beginning in the early 2000s, entrepreneurs transformed the abandoned buildings and courtyards of District VII — the old Jewish Quarter — into eclectic, art-filled bars. The concept spread worldwide; the original is still the best.

Szimpla Kert — The first and most famous ruin pub, opened in 2002. Mismatched furniture, graffiti-covered walls, found-object installations, several simultaneous bars across different rooms, and an atmosphere that’s both chaotic and strangely comfortable. A Sunday morning farmers’ market; a packed nightclub that same evening. One place, many Budapests.

Szimpla Kert — the original ruin pub, a labyrinth of mismatched furniture, graffiti, and bar counters in an abandoned District VII courtyard.

For rooftop drinking with panoramic views: 360 Bar, Leo Rooftop Bar, and The Duchess Rooftop Bar are the city’s best elevated options. For jazz and a sophisticated bite, the Opus Jazz Club at the Budapest Music Center is excellent — reserve ahead.

Coffee

Budapest has a serious specialty coffee culture layered beneath its grand old café tradition. The two coexist perfectly.

  • New York Café — Gilded, baroque, and appropriately expensive. Go once for the interior: built in 1894, with gold-leaf columns, frescoed ceilings, and red velvet banquettes. Touristy? Yes. Worth it? Also yes.
  • Café Gerbaud — One of Budapest’s oldest and most storied café-patisseries on Vörösmarty Square. A Hungarian institution for cakes and coffee.
  • Müvész (Artist) Café — On Andrássy út near the Opera House, with an old-world atmosphere and a quieter following than the famous tourist cafés.
  • Espresso Embassy — The pioneer of Budapest’s specialty coffee movement. Excellent espresso in a serious but welcoming space.
  • Fekete — Hip café with a quiet courtyard; a favorite of the city’s creative class.
  • Kelet Café — A café-library hybrid with a cozy vibe and excellent coffee. Perfect for a slow afternoon with a book.
  • My Little Melbourne — Australian-style specialty coffee done seriously, would impress in any third-wave city.

For the city’s best bakeries: Artizan (most options), Lui (more of a coffee-focused spot), and Kiskovász Kézimuves Pékség (a smaller, excellent craft bakery) are all worth seeking out.

Getting Around & Practical Notes

Budapest is easy to navigate. The metro system — one of Europe’s oldest, dating to 1896 — covers the main tourist routes efficiently, and trams run along both riverbanks. Use the BudapestGO app for public transport tickets and journey planning. Validate every ticket before boarding; inspectors are common and fines are issued on the spot.

For taxis, use Bolt, Uber, or FöTaxi — all regulated apps with transparent pricing. Never take an unlicensed taxi hailed on the street. From the airport (roughly 35 minutes, more in rush hour), expect to pay around 11,000–14,000 HUF; look for the official FöTaxi stand to the left when exiting the terminal building.

Currency: Hungary uses the Hungarian Forint (HUF), not the Euro. ATMs are widely available; most restaurants and shops accept cards, but carry some cash for markets and smaller places.

Safety: Budapest is a safe city, but standard urban cautions apply. Watch for pickpockets in tourist-heavy areas — Parliament, Castle Hill, the Chain Bridge. Be alert to tourist bar scams: establishments that produce very different menus for foreign visitors, or taxis without meters. Use your instincts.

Language: Hungarian is famously unlike any other European language. A few words — köszönöm (thank you), kérem (please), igen/nem (yes/no) — are genuinely appreciated and will earn you a warmer reception. English is widely spoken in the city center.

Shopping: The biggest shopping street is Váci utca and Fashion Street for high street brands. Andrássy út has the high-end international boutiques. The shopping malls — Westend, Arena Plaza, Mammut — are useful for practical needs.

Budapest rewards the visitor who stays long enough to fall into its rhythms: the morning coffee in a grand old café, the afternoon soak in a thermal bath, the evening walk along the Danube when the Parliament lights come on and the river goes gold. It’s a city of layers — Roman, Ottoman, Habsburg, Soviet, and now something entirely its own — and each layer reveals something worth knowing.

Go once. You’ll want to go back.
entry.summary: A traveler's guide to Budapest, where Ottoman thermal baths, ruin pubs, grand imperial boulevards, and the restless energy of a city reborn converge on the banks of the Danube.

entry.title: The Beginner’s Field Guide to Autonomous Robots

link.title: The Beginner’s Field Guide to Autonomous Robots

entry.content:
"We must shape the AI tools that will in turn shape us."
— Reid Hoffman



Source: Photo by [Kindel Media](https://www.pexels.com/photo/close-up-photo-of-delivert-robots-8566569)



Table of Contents

  • Table of Contents
  • Robot Form Factors
    • Ground Robots
    • Aerial Robots
    • Aquatic Robots
    • Industrial & Service Robots
    • Novel & Bio-Inspired Robots
  • Sensors
    • Vision and Optical Sensors
    • Motion and Orientation Sensors
    • Proximity and Distance Sensors
    • Tactile and Force Sensors
    • Environmental Sensors
    • Audio and Vibration Sensors
    • Biological and Medical Sensors
    • Specialized Application Sensors
  • Actuators: The Muscles of Robots
    • One
  • Control Systems: The Brain of Robots
    • One
  • Power Supplies
    • One
  • Robotics Theory
    • One
  • Robotics Ethics & Policy
    • One


Robot Form Factors

Ground Robots

Wheeled Robots
  • Autonomous Mobile Robot (AMR): driverless, self-navigating robots that transport materials and goods independently, using sensors and artificial intelligence (AI) to map their environments, avoid obstacles, and make real-time decisions. Examples include delivery robots, driverless cars, and space rovers.
  • Automated Guided Vehicles (AGV): portable robots that follows along marked lines, wires on the floor, or uses radio waves, vision cameras, magnets, or lasers for navigation. They are generally used in industrial applications to transport heavy materials around a factory or warehouse.
Tracked Robots
  • Continuous Tracks (CT): robots that use a single, continuous track or multiple continuous tracks for movement
  • Modular Track (MT): track systems made of individual, connected modules, offering flexibility
  • Omni Track (OT): track designs that provide omnidirectional movement capabilities, allowing the robot to move in any direction
Legged Robots
  • One-legged: pogo stick robots use a hopping motion for navigation
  • Bipeds/Humanoids: two-legged robots (e.g., Atlas, ASIMO)
  • Quadrupeds: dog-like robots (e.g., Boston Dynamics Spot)
  • Hexapods/Multi-legged: insect-inspired robots with six or more legs
Specialized and Biomorphic Robots
  • Serpentine Robot: snake-like locomotion for confined spaces. Often used for pipe inspection or rescue in tight spaces
  • Ballbot Robots: spherical design for rolling locomotion. Examples include Sphero and the Star Wars-inspired BB-8
Hybrid Robots
  • Wheeled Legs: combination of wheels and legs for versatile locomotion
  • Transformable Systems: robots that can change their form factor

Aerial Robots

Multirotors
  • Quadcopters: four-rotor drones
  • Hexacopters: six-rotor aircraft
  • Octocopters: eight-rotor heavy-lift drones
  • Microdrones: miniature flying robots, often inspired by insects.
Winged UAVs
  • Fixed-wing UAV: traditional aircraft design
  • Ornithopter: an aircraft that generates lift and thrust by flapping its wings, mimicking the flight of birds and insects (biomimetic flight)
VTOL / eVTOL
  • Electric Vertical Take-Off and Landing (eVTOL): a variety of vertical take-off and landing (VTOL) aircraft that uses electric power to hover, take off, and land vertically
Lighter-Than-Air (LTA)
  • Blimp Drone: uses a lifting gas, like helium, to provide buoyancy and an electric motor for control and propolsion. Requires less energy for lift compared to heavier-than-air drones
  • Balloon Drone: uses a lifting gas, like helium, to provide buoyancy with very limited directional control (carried by the wind). Often used for surveillance or sensing.

Aquatic Robots

Underwater Robots
  • Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs): submarine-style autonomous systems
  • Remotely Operated Underwater Vehicles (ROVs): tethered submarines controlled remotely
  • Underwater Gliders: a variable-buoyancy-driven propulsion robots for long-term missions
  • Robot Fish: a type of biomimetic robot that has the shape and locomotion of a living fish. Most use body-caudal fin (BCF) propulsion, and can be divided into three categories: single joint (SJ), multi-joint (MJ) and smart material-based "soft-body" design
Surface Vessels (USVs)
  • Robotic Boats: unmanned surface vessels. Examples include, autonomous passenger boats, cargo transport, and sensing drones

Industrial & Service Robots

Robotic Arms
  • General Manipulators: 6-DOF industrial arms for manufacturing
  • SCARA Arms: Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arms for pick-and-place
  • Delta Robots: spider-like parallel robots for high-speed packaging
Exoskeletons
  • Wearable Augmentation: human strength and endurance enhancement
  • Rehabilitation Exoskeletons: medical and therapy applications
Telepresence Robots
  • Mobile Bases with Screens: remote communication platforms
  • Video Conferencing Robots: mobile telepresence systems
Service Robots
  • Kiosk-style Assistants: information and service provision robots
  • Cleaning Bots: autonomous cleaning and maintenance robots

Novel & Bio-Inspired Robots

Soft Robots
  • Flexible Robots: Silicone-based compliant systems
  • Human-safe: Soft materials for safe human-robot interaction
Modular / Self-Reconfigurable Robots
  • Self-assembling Swarms: Robots that can reconfigure their structure
  • Modular Systems: Interchangeable robotic components
Origami Robots
  • Foldable Robots: Paper-folding inspired designs
  • Deployable Systems: Compact robots that unfold for operation


Sensors

Vision and Optical Sensors

Camera Systems
  • Monocular Camera: single camera sensor that captures 2D images for object recognition, navigation, and visual servoing. Widely used due to low cost and computational simplicity.
  • Stereo Camera: dual camera system that mimics binocular vision to provide depth perception and 3D spatial understanding through triangulation.
  • Omnidirectional Camera: 360-degree field of view cameras using fisheye lenses or multiple camera arrays for complete environmental awareness.
  • Thermal/Infrared Camera: detects heat signatures and temperature variations, useful for night vision, search and rescue, and industrial inspection.
Depth and Distance Sensors
  • LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging): uses laser pulses to create detailed 3D point clouds of the environment, essential for autonomous navigation and mapping.
  • Structured Light Sensor: projects known light patterns and analyzes deformation to calculate depth and surface geometry.
  • Time-of-Flight (ToF) Camera: measures the time light takes to travel to objects and back, providing real-time depth information.
  • Photogrammetry Sensors: extract 3D information from multiple 2D photographs taken from different angles.
Specialized Optical Sensors
  • Photodiodes: light-sensitive semiconductors used for simple light detection and optical communication.
  • Optical Flow Sensors: track visual motion patterns to estimate robot movement and velocity relative to the environment.
  • Hyperspectral Camera: captures images across multiple wavelengths for material identification and chemical analysis.

Motion and Orientation Sensors

Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs)
  • Accelerometer: measures linear acceleration in three axes, used for tilt sensing and motion detection.
  • Gyroscope: measures angular velocity and rotational motion for orientation tracking and stabilization.
  • Magnetometer: detects magnetic field strength and direction, commonly used as a digital compass.
  • 9-DOF IMU: combines accelerometer, gyroscope, and magnetometer for complete orientation sensing.
Position and Navigation Sensors
  • GPS/GNSS: satellite-based positioning system for outdoor localization and navigation.
  • Rotary Encoder: measures wheel rotation and motor shaft position for odometry and precise motion control.
  • Linear Encoder: measures linear displacement along a single axis for precise positioning systems.
  • Indoor Positioning Systems: beacon-based systems using WiFi, Bluetooth, or UWB for indoor localization.

Proximity and Distance Sensors

Ultrasonic Sensors
  • Ultrasonic Range Finder: uses sound waves to measure distance to objects, commonly used for obstacle avoidance and parking assistance.
  • Ultrasonic Arrays: multiple ultrasonic sensors arranged to provide wider coverage and improved accuracy.
Radar Sensors
  • Microwave Radar: uses radio waves to detect objects and measure distance, velocity, and angle, effective in all weather conditions.
  • Automotive Radar: specialized radar systems for vehicle collision avoidance and adaptive cruise control.
  • Millimeter Wave Radar: high-frequency radar with excellent resolution for precise object detection.
Infrared Proximity Sensors
  • PIR (Passive Infrared): detects infrared radiation from warm objects, commonly used for motion detection.
  • Active IR Proximity: emits infrared light and measures reflection for short-range distance measurement.
  • IR Break-beam Sensor: detects when objects interrupt an infrared beam between transmitter and receiver.

Tactile and Force Sensors

Touch and Pressure Sensors
  • Tactile Sensor Arrays: distributed pressure-sensitive elements that provide detailed touch information for manipulation tasks.
  • Pressure Sensors: measure applied force or pressure for grip control and material handling.
  • Piezoelectric Sensors: generate electrical signals from mechanical stress, used for vibration and impact detection.
  • Capacitive Touch Sensors: detect changes in electrical capacitance caused by touch or proximity.
Force and Torque Sensors
  • Load Cell: measures weight and force along specific axes, essential for robotic manipulation and assembly.
  • Torque Sensor: measures rotational force applied to joints and actuators for precise control.
  • 6-DOF Force/Torque Sensor: measures forces and torques in all six degrees of freedom for complex manipulation tasks.

Environmental Sensors

Chemical and Gas Sensors
  • Gas Sensors: detect specific gases for safety monitoring, environmental analysis, and leak detection.
  • pH Sensors: measure acidity/alkalinity of liquids for water quality monitoring and chemical processes.
  • VOC Sensors: detect volatile organic compounds for air quality assessment and industrial safety.
  • Smoke Detectors: specialized sensors for fire detection and safety applications.
Climate and Weather Sensors
  • Temperature Sensors: measure ambient and surface temperatures using thermocouples, RTDs, or thermistors.
  • Humidity Sensors: measure moisture content in air for environmental monitoring and control.
  • Barometric Pressure Sensors: measure atmospheric pressure for weather prediction and altitude estimation.
  • Wind Sensors: measure wind speed and direction for meteorological applications and drone flight control.

Audio and Vibration Sensors

Microphones and Audio Sensors
  • Omnidirectional Microphone: captures sound from all directions for general audio recording and voice recognition.
  • Directional Microphone: focuses on sound from specific directions to reduce background noise and improve signal quality.
  • Microphone Arrays: multiple microphones used for sound localization and beamforming.
  • Ultrasonic Microphone: detects high-frequency sounds beyond human hearing range.
Vibration and Acoustic Sensors
  • Vibration Sensors: specialized accelerometers that detect mechanical vibrations for condition monitoring.
  • Hydrophones: underwater microphones for marine robotics and sonar applications.
  • Acoustic Emission Sensors: detect stress waves in materials for structural health monitoring.

Biological and Medical Sensors

Biosensors
  • Electrochemical Biosensors: detect biological molecules through electrochemical reactions for medical diagnostics.
  • Pulse Oximeters: measure blood oxygen saturation and heart rate for health monitoring robots.
  • ECG/EKG Sensors: monitor electrical activity of the heart for medical assistance robots.
  • EMG Sensors: detect muscle electrical activity for prosthetic control and rehabilitation robots.

Specialized Application Sensors

Industrial and Inspection Sensors
  • Eddy Current Sensors: non-destructive testing sensors that detect flaws in conductive materials.
  • X-ray Sensors: penetrating radiation sensors for internal inspection and quality control.
  • Laser Displacement Sensors: high-precision distance measurement for manufacturing and inspection.
  • Current Sensors: monitor electrical current flow in robotic systems for safety and control.
Multi-modal and Fusion Sensors
  • RGB-D Cameras: combine color and depth information in a single sensor package.
  • Sensor Fusion Systems: integrate multiple sensor types for enhanced perception and reliability.
  • Event-based Cameras: asynchronous vision sensors that respond to changes in brightness rather than capturing frames.


Actuators: The Muscles of Robots

One


Control Systems: The Brain of Robots

One


Power Supplies

One


Robotics Theory

One


Robotics Ethics & Policy

One
entry.summary: An atlas to self-guided machines and mechatronics

entry.title: AI Agent Memory
entry.content:
"What we call the present is given shape by an accumulation of memories."
— Haruki Murakami



Why Do Agents Need Memory

Imagine trying to hold a conversation with someone who forgets everything you’ve said the moment you stop talking. That’s essentially what AI agents are without memory, perpetually amnesiac, doomed to reinvent the wheel with every interaction.

Memory gives agents context: what you asked before, what actions they’ve taken, and what worked (or failed) in the past. It’s the difference between your trusted lieutenance and right hand man and a goldfish with Wi-Fi.

Just like humans, agents use memory to build up knowledge, learn from past mistakes, and recognize familiar faces. This helps them better personalize experiences, conduct long-term planning, and avoids repeat mistakes. Without memory, an agent can’t improve or adapt; it’s like trying to navigate a city with no map and no recollection of where you’ve been. Memory turns reactive automatons into proactive thinkers.

What is AI Agent Memory

  • An agent’s “memory” is data that is not provided by the user in their prompt, but is retrieved and appended to the reasoning process via runtime calls.
  • Agent memory encompasses a diverse set of references and can include everything from past user interactions, previous agent actions, external knowledge bases, system prompts, guardrails, etc.
  • The additional context and knowledge provided by memory helps the agent to better conceptualize the request, plan, and then answer the user or take an action.


Source: Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents


Long-Term Agent Memory Types

  1. Episodic: this type of memory contains past agent interactions and agent action logs. For example, if you asked a chatbot to “repeat the last action that it took in a previous session,” it could use episodic memory to complete this request.
  2. Semantic: this type of memory contains any knowledge the agent should have about itself, or any grounding information stored in knowledge bases that the agent has access to. For example, a vector store in a RAG application is semantic memory.
  3. Procedural: this type of memory contains system information like the system prompt, metadata on available tools, guardrails, etc. It is usually stored and versioned in Git or prompt registry tools.

Short-Term Agent Memory

  • Any of the above long-term memory types that is pulled during runtime is called “short-term memory” or “working memory”.
  • This short-term memory is added to the user prompt and passed to the LLM with the aim of boosting performance
  • Any intermediate reasoning steps/action history of the current session is also considered short-term memory when in-use.


Source: SwirlAI


References

  • Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents
  • SwirlAI
entry.summary: Demystifying the different types of AI agent memory

entry.title: The Roadmap to AI Product Manager

link.title: The Roadmap to AI Product Manager

entry.content:
"Be stubborn on vision but flexible on details."
— Jeff Bezos


Source: Photo by Denise Jans on Unsplash


What Makes an AI Product Manager

I frequently get asked by product managers, students, and engineers I work with, “how can I upskill on AI and learn AI product management”. The AI landscape is moving at breakneck speed with new models, frameworks, and capabilities emerging weekly, making it challenging to know where to start or what to prioritize. This guide provides a structured survey of the essential skills needed to excel as an AI PM, organized by domain and prioritized for practical application.

So how does an AI Product Manager differ from a traditional PM? In general, traditional PMs build scalable systems with stable, time-tested tools in a largely deterministic environment. AI product management, on the other hand, often feels like riding a tiger, with the landscape shifting faster than you can map it. Your tools are often weeks old, cutting-edge research is part of the day job, and the products you’re guiding are probabilistic by nature, behaving differently depending on the data they see and often exhibiting jagged edges of performance. Success isn’t measured in features shipped, but in nuanced metrics like model quality, inference efficiency, and real-world generalization. And just when you think you’ve found your footing, a new framework, model, or chip drops, because the open-source community and AI research labs are working 996’s and never stop shipping.

The development process also differs dramatically. Instead of the generally linear Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC), AI systems require a a blend of linear SDLC and highly iterative Model Development Lifecycle (MDLC). This involves continuously tweaking model training pipelines, feature stores, real-time inference latency, versioning of data and models, and monitoring for drift and degradation—all while coordinating with a diverse team of data scientists, ML engineers, infrastructure teams, data SMEs, end users, and model governance stakeholders. This guide maps out the skills you’ll need to navigate this complex, fast-moving landscape successfully.


Skill Sets

Traditional Data Science

A strong foundation in traditional data science is essential for AI PMs because it provides the fundamental language and concepts needed to communicate effectively with data scientists and ML engineers. Understanding probability, statistics, machine learning theory, and evaluation metrics enables PMs to make informed decisions about model selection, set realistic performance expectations, and translate technical capabilities into business value. Without this knowledge, PMs risk making promises that can’t be delivered, misinterpreting model performance, or failing to identify when simpler statistical or deterministic methods might be more appropriate than complex ML or Gen AI solutions.

Probability & Statistics
  • Fundamental Concepts
    • Probability distributions (normal, binomial, Poisson, exponential)
    • Conditional probability and Bayes' theorem
    • Expected value, variance, and standard deviation
    • Central Limit Theorem
    • Law of Large Numbers
  • Statistical Inference
    • Hypothesis testing (null hypothesis, p-values, significance levels)
    • Confidence intervals
    • Type I and Type II errors
    • Statistical power
    • Bayesian vs. frequentist approaches
  • Descriptive Statistics
    • Measures of central tendency (mean, median, mode)
    • Measures of dispersion (range, variance, standard deviation, IQR)
    • Skewness and kurtosis
    • Percentiles and quartiles
  • Correlation and Causation
    • Understanding correlation coefficients (Pearson, Spearman)
    • Distinguishing correlation from causation
    • Confounding variables
    • Spurious correlations
  • Sampling Methods
    • Random sampling
    • Stratified sampling
    • Cluster sampling
    • Bias in sampling
    • Sample size determination
Machine Learning Theory
  • Supervised vs. unsupervised learning
  • Regression, classification, clustering, association, and dimensionality reduction
  • Familiarity with statistics and ML models and when to use one over the other
    • Linear Regression
    • Logistic Regression
    • Random Forest
    • Support Vector Machines (SVM)
    • eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost)
    • K-means Clustering
    • Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM)
    • Categorical Boosting (CatBoost)
  • Model hyperparameters
  • Grid vs. random search (gradient descent)
  • Loss functions
  • Overfitting and underfitting
  • Bias and variance
  • Model evaluation techniques (train/test split, cross validation, metrics)
Deep Learning Theory
  • Model architectures for Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and deep learning models (DNN, CNN, RNN, etc.)
    • Activation functions
      • Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU)
        • Leaky ReLU
        • Softplus/SmoothReLU
        • Parametric ReLU (PReLU)
        • Exponential Linear Unit (ELU)
        • Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU)
      • Sigmoid
    • Loss functions/cost functions
      • SME
    • Optimizers
      • Adadelta
      • Adafactor
      • Adaptive Gradient Algorithm (AdaGrad)
      • Adaptive Moment Estimation (Adam)
      • Adam with Weight Decay (AdamW)
      • Adamax
      • Follow-the-Regularized-Leader (Ftrl)
      • Lion
      • LossScaleOptimizer
      • Nesterov-accelerated Adaptive Moment Estimation (Nadam)
      • Root Mean Square Propagation (RMSprop)
      • Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
    • Backpropagation
    • Epochs
    • Learning Rate
    • Batch Size
    • Regularization
      • Early Stopping
      • Parameter Norm Penalties
        • L1 Regularization
        • L2 Regularization
        • Max-norm Regularization
      • Dataset Augmentation
      • Noise Robustness
      • Sparse Representations
    • Model weights and biases
    • Embedding dimensionality: Lower dimensions can lead to less accuracy, more lossy compression. Higher can lead to overfitting and slow training time. A good starting point for the number of embedding dimensions is Dimensions \(\approx \sqrt[4]{\mathrm{Possible\ values}}\)
AI Evaluation Metrics
  • Theory
    • Build an intuitive understanding of the right metrics for each model and use case, especially trade-offs.
    • Recognize areas of concern or blind spots for each metric.
    • Consider business context when selecting evaluation metrics (e.g., cost of false positives vs. false negatives).
  • Regression Eval Metrics & Techniques
    • Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
    • Mean Squared Error (MSE)
    • Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
    • Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)
    • R-squared (R²) / Coefficient of Determination
    • Adjusted R-squared
    • Mean Bias Error (MBE)
  • Classification Eval Metrics & Techniques
    • Accuracy
    • Precision
    • Recall (Sensitivity)
    • F1-Score (harmonic mean of precision and recall)
    • F-beta Score (weighted F1 for precision/recall trade-offs)
    • Specificity (True Negative Rate)
    • Confusion Matrix
    • Precision-Recall Curve
    • Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve
    • Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC-ROC)
    • Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUC-PR)
    • Log Loss (Logarithmic Loss)
    • Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)
  • Multi-class Classification Metrics
    • Macro-averaged metrics (precision, recall, F1)
    • Micro-averaged metrics
    • Weighted-averaged metrics
    • Per-class metrics
  • Imbalanced Dataset Metrics
    • When to use precision vs. recall vs. F1-score
    • Precision-Recall AUC (often better than ROC-AUC for imbalanced data)
    • Balanced Accuracy
    • Cohen's Kappa
  • Clustering Metrics
    • Silhouette Score
    • Davies-Bouldin Index
    • Calinski-Harabasz Index
    • Inertia (within-cluster sum of squares)
    • Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) - for labeled data
    • Normalized Mutual Information (NMI) - for labeled data
  • Evaluation Techniques
    • Train/Test Split
    • K-Fold Cross-Validation
    • Stratified K-Fold Cross-Validation
    • Time Series Cross-Validation (for temporal data)
    • Bootstrap Sampling
    • Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV)
    • Holdout Validation
  • Model Performance Beyond Metrics
    • Inference latency and throughput
    • Model size and memory requirements
    • Training time and computational cost
    • Robustness to adversarial examples
    • Fairness and bias metrics
    • Explainability and interpretability scores
ML & Deep Learning Frameworks
  • Scikit-Learn
  • PyTorch
  • TensorFlow
  • JAX
  • Resources
    • (Class) Deep Learning Specialisation by Andrew Ng
    • (Videos) Neural Networks: Zero to Hero by Andrej Karpathy
Model Development Lifecycle (MDLC)
  • Understand the end-to-end MDLC process of building, testing, deploying, monitoring, and decommissioning machine learning models.
Machine Learning Operations (MLOps)
  • Learn the principles and practices of maintaining and scaling ML workflows in production environments.
ML Pipelines
  • Pipeline Phases
    • Data Preparation
      • Data Extraction
      • Data Analysis
      • Data Preparation
    • Model Development
      • Model Training
      • Model Evaluation
      • Model Validation
    • Model Serving
      • Model Registry
      • Model Prediction
      • Model Performance Monitoring
Python
  • Programming paradigms: Functional and Object-oriented programming (OOP)
  • Data manipulation: Pandas, NumPy, PySpark, Koalas
  • Package management: uv, pyproject.toml
  • Documentation: README.md, Agents.md
  • Testing & Linting: pytest, ruff
SQL
  • Ensure fluency in querying and manipulating structured data from relational databases.
Distributed Computing & Big Data Processing
  • Leverage distributed computing for large-scale data processing.
  • Use PySpark for writing scalable, Python-based ETL and analysis pipelines.
Data Warehouses & Lakehouses
  • Databricks
  • Snowflake
  • GCP BigQuery
  • Amazon Redshift
  • Azure Synapse Analytics


Gen AI & Foundation Models

As generative AI and foundation models become central to modern AI products, PMs must understand their capabilities, limitations, costs, and operational requirements. Knowledge of transformer architectures, scaling laws, and fine-tuning techniques allows PMs to make strategic decisions about when to use pre-trained models versus building custom solutions, estimate compute costs and infrastructure needs, and set appropriate expectations for solution performance with executives. This expertise is crucial for navigating the rapidly evolving landscape of foundation models and ensuring products leverage the latest advances while maintaining cost efficiency and reliability.

Transformer Theory
  • Architecture & modeling
    • Attention mechanism
    • Positional encoding
    • Tokenization & vector embeddings
    • Decoder-only, encoder-only, and encoder-decoder architectures
    • Key-Value (KV) Cache
    • Hyperparameters
      • Temperature
      • Top-K
      • Top-P
    • Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA)
    • Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)
  • Resources:
    • (Video) Deep Dive into LLMs like ChatGPT by Andrej Karpathy
    • (Paper) Attention is All You Need
    • (Class) Stanford CS229 - Machine Learning - Building Large Language Models (LLMs)
Gen AI Scaling Laws
  • Pre-training Scaling (parameter, data, and training compute size)
  • Post-training Scaling (RLHF)
  • Test-time Scaling/Inference Scaling
  • Multi-Agent Scaling
Diffusion Models
  • Fundamentals
    • Forward diffusion process (adding noise to data)
    • Reverse diffusion process (denoising to generate samples)
    • Noise schedule and timesteps
    • Score-based generative models
  • Architecture & Techniques
    • Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM)
    • Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM)
    • Latent Diffusion Models (LDM)
    • Stable Diffusion architecture
    • Classifier-free guidance
    • Conditional generation (text-to-image, image-to-image)
  • Applications
    • Image generation
    • Text-to-image synthesis
    • Image inpainting and editing
    • Super-resolution
    • Audio generation
    • Video generation
  • Training & Optimization
    • Training objectives (variational lower bound, score matching)
    • Sampling strategies (DDPM, DDIM, DPM-Solver)
    • Inference speed optimization
    • Distillation techniques
  • Resources
    • (Blog) What are Diffusion Models? by Lilian Weng
    • (Video) How Diffusion Models Work by AI Jason
Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)
  • Fundamentals
    • Generator and discriminator networks
    • Adversarial training process
    • Minimax game formulation
    • Nash equilibrium in GAN training
    • Loss functions (adversarial loss, feature matching)
  • Architecture Variants
    • Deep Convolutional GAN (DCGAN)
    • Progressive GAN (ProGAN)
    • StyleGAN and StyleGAN2
    • Conditional GAN (cGAN)
    • Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)
    • Least Squares GAN (LSGAN)
    • CycleGAN (for image-to-image translation)
    • Pix2Pix
  • Training Challenges & Solutions
    • Mode collapse
    • Training instability
    • Vanishing gradients
    • Non-convergence issues
    • Techniques for stable training (spectral normalization, gradient penalty)
  • Applications
    • Image generation
    • Image-to-image translation
    • Data augmentation
    • Super-resolution
  • Evaluation Metrics
    • Inception Score (IS)
    • Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)
    • Kernel Inception Distance (KID)
    • Precision and Recall for distributions
  • Resources
    • (Course) DeepLearning.AI - GAN Specialization
    • (Paper) Original GAN Paper (Goodfellow et al.)
    • (Paper) Wasserstein GAN by Arjovsky et al.
    • (Tutorial) TensorFlow Deep Convolutional GAN Tutorial
    • (Video) A Friendly Introduction to GANs by Serrano Academy
Foundation Model Fine-Tuning
  • Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)
    • Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)
    • QLoRA (Quantized LoRA)
    • Adapter modules (Houlsby, Pfeiffer)
    • Prefix tuning
    • Prompt tuning
    • P-tuning / P-tuning v2
    • IA³ (Infused Adapter by Inhibiting and Amplifying)
  • Quantization
    • Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation (QLoRA)
    • Post-training quantization (PTQ)
    • Quantization-aware training (QAT)
    • INT8 / INT4 / binary quantization
    • GPTQ (GPT Quantization)
    • AWQ (Activation-aware Weight Quantization)
    • SmoothQuant
    • BitsAndBytes
  • Pruning
    • Structured vs. unstructured pruning
    • Magnitude-based pruning
    • Movement pruning
    • Wanda (Weight and Activation pruning)
    • LLM-Pruner and similar methods
  • Knowledge Distillation
    • Teacher–student distillation
    • Response distillation
    • Feature / representation distillation
  • Resources
    • (Paper) LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models by Hu et al.
    • (Paper) QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs by Dettmers et al.
    • (Library) Hugging Face PEFT
Foundation Model Benchmarks
  • Fundamentals
    • What benchmarks measure (knowledge, reasoning, safety, coding, etc.)
    • Leaderboards vs. arenas vs. LLM-as-judge
    • Limitations of benchmarks (data contamination, narrow tasks, overfitting)
    • When to trust benchmarks vs. real-world evaluation
  • General Model Knowledge & Capability
    • MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding)
    • HellaSwag
    • TruthfulQA
    • ARC (AI2 Reasoning Challenge)
    • OpenBookQA
    • Winogrande
    • PIQA (Physical IQ)
  • Reasoning Benchmarks
    • GSM8K (math word problems)
    • MATH
    • HumanEval (code)
    • Big-Bench (BIG-Bench Hard)
    • DROP (discrete reasoning)
    • CommonsenseQA
    • StrategyQA
  • Coding Benchmarks
    • HumanEval
    • MBPP (Mostly Basic Python Programming)
    • DS-1000
    • SWE-bench
    • CodeContests
    • MultiPL-E
  • Safety & Alignment
    • TruthfulQA (truthfulness)
    • RealToxicityPrompts
    • BBH (BIG-Bench Hard) safety subsets
    • Red-teaming and adversarial benchmarks
  • Multimodal & Long-Context
    • MMMU, MMMU-Pro
    • ChartQA, DocVQA
    • Long-context benchmarks (Needle in a Haystack, etc.)
  • Aggregate Suites & Leaderboards
    • Open LLM Leaderboard (Hugging Face)
    • LMSys Chatbot Arena
    • MT-Bench
    • AlpacaEval
    • HELM (Holistic Evaluation of Language Models)
  • Resources
    • (Podcast) AI Fundamentals: Benchmarks 101
    • (Podcast) Benchmarks 201: Why Leaderboards > Arenas >> LLM-as-Judge
    • (Leaderboard) Open LLM Leaderboard
    • (Arena) LMSys Chatbot Arena
    • (Paper) Holistic Evaluation of Language Models (HELM)
    • (Suite) OpenCompass Evaluation
Foundation Model Serving
  • Cloud Model APIs
    • Azure - AI Foundry
    • GCP - Model Garden
    • AWS - Amazon Bedrock
    • OpenAI
  • Local Serving
    • Ollama
Foundation Model Evaluation
  • LLM evaluation metrics
    • Statistical metrics
      • BLEU
      • ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation)
      • METEOR (Metric for Evaluation of Translation with Explicit Ordering)
      • Levenshtein Distance
    • LLM-as-judge metrics
  • Evaluation datasets
    • Golden dataset
    • Adversarial dataset
    • Regression dataset
  • LLM evaluation tools
    • Ragas
    • OpenAI Evals
  • LLM-as-a-judge techniques
    • Pairwise comparison
    • Evaluation by criteria (reference free)
    • Evaluation by criteria (reference-based)
Foundation Model Observability
  • Langfuse
  • LangSmith: a developer platform for inspecting, tracing, and evaluating LLM-powered applications built with LangChain or other orchestration frameworks. It enables fine-grained logging of prompts, model inputs/outputs, tool invocations, and intermediate steps, while supporting automated and manual evaluation workflows for performance, latency, and correctness.
AI Interpretability
  • Interpretability Methods
    • Post-hoc Explainability
    • Intrinsic Interpretability
    • Mechanistic Interpretability
  • Anthropic's Interpretability Team Publications
    • Dictionary Learning
    • Monosemanticity
    • Attributional Graphs
Foundation Model Safety, Security, & Guardrails
  • Content filtering
    • GCP Model Armor
    • Prompt injection
Prompt/Context Engineering
  • Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting
  • ReAct
  • Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) Prompting
  • (Article) OpenAI Prompting Guide
  • (Website) Prompt Engineering Guide by DAIR.AI


Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)

RAG has become the dominant pattern for building production LLM applications that need access to private or up-to-date information. PMs with RAG expertise can design systems that effectively combine retrieval and generation, choose appropriate vector databases and embedding strategies, and establish evaluation frameworks that measure both retrieval quality and response relevance. Understanding RAG is essential for building or evaluating AI products that go beyond simple chatbot interfaces to create intelligent systems that can reason over large knowledge bases and provide accurate, contextualized responses.

RAG Fundamentals
  • Vector embeddings
  • Chunking
  • Hybrid retrieval
  • General resources
    • (GitHub) RAG Techniques by Nir Diamant
RAG Evaluation
  • RAG evaluation metrics
    • Context Precision
    • Context Recall
    • Content Entities Recall
    • Noise Sensitivity
    • Response Relevance
    • Faithfulness
    • Multimodal Faithfulness
    • Multimodal Relevance
  • RAG Eval Tools
    • Ragas
    • DeepEval
Vector Databases
  • Vector search prototyping libraries
    • FAISS
    • HNSWlib
  • Production databases
    • Pinecone
    • Weaviate
    • Chroma
    • Elasticsearch
    • Milvus
Agentic RAG


Agentic AI

Agentic AI represents the next evolution of AI systems, moving from single-turn interactions to autonomous agents that can plan, reason, and take actions across multiple steps. PMs need to understand agent frameworks, design patterns, and evaluation methods to build products that can handle complex, multi-step workflows. This knowledge is critical for designing agent architectures that balance autonomy with control, ensuring agents can operate safely and effectively in production environments while delivering on ambitious product visions that require sophisticated reasoning and tool use.

Agent Fundamentals
  • Resources
    • (White Paper) Google Agents White Paper by Julia Wiesinger et al.
    • (White Paper) Google Agents Companion by Antonio Gulli et al.
    • (Paper) AI Agents vs. Agentic AI: A Conceptual Taxonomy, Applications and Challenges
    • (Paper) ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models by Shunyu Yao et al.
    • (Course) HuggingFace AI Agents Course
Agent Evaluation
  • Resources
    • (Paper) Agent-as-a-Judge: Evaluate Agents with Agents
Agent Frameworks
  • LangChain: open-source framework for building agents
  • LangGraph: a newer-graph-based library within the LangChain ecosystem designed for creating complex, stateful, and multi-agent workflows with explicit state management and the ability to handle loops and cycles.
    • (Class) AI Agents in LangGraph by DeepLearning.AI
  • LlamaIndex: specializes in efficiently indexing and querying large datasets for Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) applications
    • (Class) Building Agentic RAG with LlamaIndex
  • OpenAI Agent SDK: OpenAI's framework for building agentic AI apps in a lightweight, easy-to-use package with few abstractions. It's a production-ready upgrade of their previous experimentatal framework (Swarm).
  • Google Agent Development Kit (ADK)
    • (Code Lab) ADK Crash Course - From Beginner To Expert
Agent Protocols
  • Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (MCP)
  • Google's Agent-2-Agent (A2A)
  • Google's Agent Payments Protocol (AP2)
AI Coding Agents, CLIs, & IDEs
  • Cursor
